gist: Jordy Visser tells Anthony Pompliano the 'AI midcycle slowdown' is over and that the memory/semiconductor selloff was a leverage flush rather than a bubble bursting, while separately calling a Bitcoin bottom on a fresh RSI divergence and expecting crypto to become 'the memory trade of next year' as fundamental managers rotate in.

insights.claim: Meta and XAI's aggressive AI pricing/affordability announcements are an attempt to keep enterprise customers from migrating to open-source alternatives, not evidence that they can catch up to OpenAI and Anthropic on frontier model capability.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Don't read Meta/XAI price cuts as sector-wide AI margin compression; OpenAI and Anthropic's enterprise business remains largely insulated from this specific price war.
insights.quote: I don't think anyone anymore can catch up to anthropic and open AAI and what their plans are.

insights.claim: Meta, Apple, Google, and Amazon are pursuing a different business model from OpenAI/Anthropic centered on consumer agents, advertising, and personal assistants, while Amazon, Google, and Microsoft additionally run cloud/compute-rental businesses (e.g., renting compute to SpaceX).
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Value these companies on advertising/commerce ROIC from consumer agents rather than on frontier-model quality, since they've effectively ceded the model race to OpenAI and Anthropic.

insights.claim: Enterprises are restructuring how they call AI models by routing simple queries to cheap models and complex ones to expensive frontier models, which should cut aggregate AI compute spend even as total usage keeps rising and could accelerate open-source adoption.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano, host
insights.so_what: A drop in cost-per-query doesn't signal falling AI capex demand; track token volume growth outpacing dollar spend as the real signal of a healthy market.
insights.quote: how do you actually start to route queries to different models based on the complexity or the compute need of that query.

insights.claim: Semianalysis founder Dylan Patel's firm increased AI spend from roughly $100,000 a year in December to an $11 million annualized run-rate, and argues any company cutting AI budget out of cost concern is signaling competitive decline.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest (relaying Dylan Patel)
insights.so_what: Rising, not falling, AI opex is becoming a competitive necessity — a useful screen for identifying which companies are under- vs. over-investing in AI.
insights.quote: if you're not spending more money every year, you're going out of business.

insights.claim: Startups have a structural AI-adoption advantage over large incumbents like Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley because they aren't constrained by legacy payroll and can build AI-native workflows from scratch.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Expect AI-native smaller companies to out-execute large incumbents on margin expansion from AI adoption over the next few years.

themes.theme: AI Midcycle Slowdown Ending, Consumer Agents Repricing Apple & Meta

insights.claim: The market has become too negative on the AI buildout, and the 'AI midcycle slowdown' is now over, with the next leg driven by consumer agents repricing companies like Apple and Meta and by agentic commerce flowing into crypto.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Investors who cut AI infrastructure/hyperscaler exposure on slowdown fears may be underweighting a re-acceleration; watch for Apple and Meta re-rating as confirmation.
insights.quote: I'm calling the the AI midcycle slowdown is over

insights.claim: Apple's stock hit all-time highs on its Siri/personal-intelligence announcement before selling off, then fell over 10% off highs on memory-driven price-increase news, then fully recovered on no news — a pattern read as a bullish reaction to bad news.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: The 'sells off on bad news but doesn't follow through lower' pattern is a technical tell that positioning is already shifting bullish ahead of a fundamental re-rating.
insights.quote: This is not a cheap stock. This tells me this is a bullish reaction to bad news.

insights.claim: Visser expects Apple to be repriced higher on consumer-agent ROIC over the coming months and predicts the market narrative a year from now will be 'how did we miss Apple,' but he is not yet convinced on Meta because its technical picture is weaker.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Differentiate within mega-cap AI laggards: Apple looks like the higher-conviction re-rating candidate versus Meta near-term.

insights.claim: A Goldman Sachs report estimates consumer AI agents will consume roughly 30 times the compute of coding agents, implying there is no near-term glut in AI compute capacity.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: This directly counters the 'AI compute overcapacity' bear case; expect a fresh demand leg for compute-adjacent infrastructure as consumer agents scale.
insights.quote: consumer agents will consume about 30 times 30 times what coding agents will consume from a compute basis.

insights.claim: Voice assistants like Siri and Alexa underperform because they require the user to actively open an app, whereas always-on, low-latency contexts like Grok inside a Tesla show much better conversational performance, suggesting hardware-embedded, always-listening devices are the stronger consumer-agent form factor.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Watch which companies pair AI assistants with always-on hardware (cars, wearables) as a leading indicator of consumer-agent product-market fit.

themes.theme: Memory Trade: Leverage Flush, Not a Bubble

insights.claim: Visser sold his Micron position near its highs around $1,250-1,300 specifically because leverage had become visibly obvious across Korean investors, macro strategies, and fundamental long-short funds, even while remaining structurally bullish on the memory trade.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Rising, visible leverage/positioning crowding is a sell signal independent of the underlying fundamental thesis; re-entry may require buying back above the exit price.

insights.claim: Visser expects Micron to reach $4,000-5,000 before the memory cycle plays out, but believes the climb from current levels near $900-950 will be harder than the prior run from $100 to $500.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: The long-term bullish memory thesis is intact, but expect 30-40% drawdowns and choppier trading rather than a smooth compounding move.

insights.claim: Samsung is projected to earn roughly $217 billion this year alone, more than its combined earnings over the prior 40 years, which Visser cites as evidence the memory rally reflects real earnings power rather than a bubble.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Judge whether AI-memory names are overextended using realized/forward earnings power rather than price action alone.

insights.claim: Samsung's roughly 20% drawdown over a 10-day period has happened only twice before in 25 years — during the COVID crash and the post-Lehman crisis — yet the stock remains near all-time highs, indicating the selloff was a leverage/positioning flush rather than a fundamental repricing.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: A rare-magnitude drawdown alongside record earnings and a price still near highs argues for buying the memory-sector dip rather than treating it as a top.

insights.claim: A leveraged 2x semiconductor ETF in Hong Kong fell 62%, and SK Hynix's oversubscribed ADR issuance was dominated by long-only managers who had missed the earlier rally — both signs that speculative leverage has been meaningfully washed out of the memory trade.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Capitulation-style buying from previously underexposed long-only funds after a leverage flush often marks a more durable entry point than the earlier leveraged rally.

insights.claim: Visser identifies the first bullish RSI divergence on Bitcoin's 4-hour chart since the cycle peak — price made a new low breaking below $60,000 while RSI printed higher than at the prior low — and is using it as a trigger to buy above $60k with a stop below the recent lows.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: A concrete, replicable technical trigger for tactical Bitcoin entries; $60k is the pivot level to watch.

insights.claim: Visser believes Bitcoin is near the bottom of its range for the next year — a dip to $45,000-50,000 wouldn't surprise him — but expects it to trade above $100,000 within a year, making near-term entry price a secondary concern relative to the longer-term move.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: On a 12-month horizon, exact entry timing is less important than positioning for a large asymmetric move higher.
insights.quote: Do I think we'll be over 100 a year from now? Yeah.

insights.claim: Capital rotating into AI-linked equities like Micron (a roughly 20-bagger) pulled money out of Bitcoin and crypto broadly, since investors chase whatever asset class is moving fastest rather than sticking to a single theme.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Bitcoin's underperformance reflected a capital-rotation effect, not crypto-specific weakness; a cooling in AI-stock momentum could rotate capital back into Bitcoin.

insights.claim: Bitcoin's decline coincided with the release of Anthropic's Opus 4.5 around October and a shift in Fed rate expectations, as roughly 150 basis points of priced-in rate cuts as of late September evaporated and were partly replaced with priced-in hike risk.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Bitcoin's recent price action has been more macro-rate-driven than crypto-native; track Fed pricing as a leading indicator for Bitcoin direction.

insights.claim: Visser assigns a 35-40% probability the Fed raises rates at the July 29 FOMC meeting; if it does not hike, he expects Bitcoin to trade above $70,000 as markets price out further hikes ahead of the midterms.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: The July 29 FOMC decision is a concrete near-term catalyst and level to watch for a potential Bitcoin breakout above $70k.
insights.quote: There's a possibility, a good possibility, it's like 35 to 40%, that they will. If they don't, then I think Bitcoin will be above 70,000 when that time comes.

insights.claim: A Fed official Visser referred to in conversation as 'Walsh' has signaled AI could be a productivity boom that's briefly inflationary before turning deflationary, and Visser argues the Fed's last preference is a tightening cycle heading into the midterms given its political origin.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: A dovish reaction function around AI-driven disinflation reduces near-term hike risk, which is supportive for Bitcoin and other risk assets.

insights.claim: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's speech at the New York Economic Club explicitly named digital assets, tokenization, and stablecoins as central to the administration's strategy to keep the US in control of global financial rails.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Policy support for stablecoins and tokenization is a structural tailwind investors should weight into crypto allocations, separate from short-term price timing.
insights.quote: digital assets, tokenization, stable coins are all part of the administration's focus on this new economic order.

insights.claim: Bitcoin has traded as a high-beta hedge within the software-momentum trade, grouped alongside Salesforce, Adobe, and Workday as vehicles funds used to hedge semiconductor exposure, meaning its recent weakness reflected a momentum-unwind effect rather than crypto-specific rejection.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: As momentum/quant deleveraging completes, the same correlation mechanism that dragged Bitcoin down should support a rebound.
insights.quote: All rallies that last for a year start with short covering.

insights.claim: Visser's Bitcoin bull thesis plays out in stages: first short covering, then a durable momentum shift once price reclaims the 200-day moving average, currently around $76,000-77,000 and roughly 20% above spot.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: The 200-day moving average (~$76-77k) is the technical level to watch for confirmation the rally has shifted from short-covering to a durable uptrend.

insights.claim: Michael Saylor's two large Bitcoin sales drew a muted market reaction, with Bitcoin trading higher after his largest-ever sale than before it, which Visser reads as a bullish signal rather than a warning sign, and expects the Saylor narrative to become irrelevant within a year.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Don't treat Saylor-related headline risk as a structural threat to Bitcoin; the market's failure to sell off on his largest sale is itself a bullish tell.

insights.claim: Fundamental and generalist fund managers have not yet rotated into Bitcoin or stablecoins because they remain focused on AI positioning, but Visser expects that once Fed clarity emerges, crypto will become 'the memory trade' of the next year.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Positioning ahead of generalist-fund rotation into crypto could capture the next multi-month momentum leg, similar to how the AI-memory trade played out this past year.
insights.quote: crypto will be the memory for next year.

themes.theme: Beyond AI & Crypto: Healthcare, Insurance, Regional Banks

insights.claim: Visser holds Eli Lilly as a high-conviction, essentially permanent position, citing its 'sovereign AI' compute buildout (he references roughly a thousand Nvidia Blackwell GPUs on its campus) applied directly to biotech drug discovery.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Owning in-house AI compute is becoming a competitive moat in biotech/pharma, not just at hyperscalers — a screen for picking AI-adjacent healthcare names.
insights.quote: I don't think even if it were to go up, even if it were a threebagger from here, I wouldn't get out of it.

insights.claim: Regional and midsize banks are likely acquisition targets for consolidation because they are hard to take private under banking regulation, and acquirers can make deals immediately accretive by infusing AI and cutting headcount post-merger.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Watch for M&A premiums building in midsize regional bank names as AI-driven cost synergies make roll-ups newly attractive.

insights.claim: Insurance and healthcare companies that have already invested in AI/agentic workflows are positioned to show visible earnings benefits over the next year, alongside banks broadly, several of which have already made new all-time highs.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: A sector-rotation opportunity into AI-adoption laggards-turned-early-adopters outside the mega-cap tech complex.

themes.theme: Commodities: Gold, Silver, and Range-Bound Oil

insights.claim: Visser expects gold and silver to rally alongside his Bitcoin thesis, driven by AI capital having pulled dollars out of these assets more than realized, rather than a resurgence of the classic dollar-debasement trade.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Precious metals could be a correlated proxy trade on the same 'AI capital suck reversing' thesis driving the Bitcoin call.

insights.claim: If the Fed hikes rates in July, Visser expects it to be a one-and-done 'credibility hike' that pushes long-end yields down and ultimately acts as a positive catalyst for risk assets including gold, silver, and Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: A near-term hike need not be bearish if read as a credibility move capping future tightening; watch the long end of the yield curve for confirmation.

insights.claim: Despite the on-again/off-again Iran conflict, crude oil hasn't sustained a move higher and trades as if there's a supply glut; Visser expects it to fall back toward $50 once the conflict resolves and sees it range-bound between $65-80 barring a Strait of Hormuz disruption.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Fade geopolitical oil-spike fears absent an actual Hormuz shipping disruption or a break above $80; the investment community has largely stopped pricing in Iran-war risk.
insights.quote: unless we get above 80 and the Iran situation is worsening where there's no ships going through Hormuz, I just don't think it matters for the markets.

insights.claim: Crack spreads — the price differential between gasoline and crude — remain wide even as crude is flat, indicating the bottleneck is refining/conversion capacity for gasoline rather than crude supply itself.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Refining-capacity-exposed names may retain pricing power even if crude oil itself stays range-bound.

themes.theme: Robotics & the AI Compute Supply-Demand Mismatch

insights.claim: 1X's robotic hand demo, built on a synthetic tendon-based mechanism rather than traditional robotic joints, demonstrated fine dexterity such as screwing in a lightbulb and picking a cherry without crushing it, which both speakers view as a major leap likely developed with AI assistance.
insights.speaker: Anthony Pompliano, host
insights.so_what: The historically hardest unsolved problem in robotics, dexterous hands, may be inflecting now, a leading indicator for humanoid-robot investment timelines.

insights.claim: The ROIC risk for AI hyperscalers is not comparable to the fracking bust because oil demand grows only linearly with GDP and is physically capped, whereas AI/intelligence usage grows exponentially as token prices fall, with the only real constraint being physical buildout like data centers and gas turbines.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts the 'AI capex is the next fracking-style bust' bear case circulating among hedge fund investors; Visser sees a persistent supply/demand mismatch, not overbuild.
insights.quote: Oil is oil. Intelligence is everything.

insights.claim: Visser believes AI-driven recursive self-improvement across model generations will eventually help solve fundamental science and physics problems, including energy generation via fission/fusion, to the point that he expects crude oil's economic value to eventually go to zero.
insights.speaker: Jordy Visser, guest
insights.so_what: A long-horizon framework for why energy-transition and fusion-adjacent bets could benefit from the same AI-compounding thesis driving equity markets today.
insights.quote: eventually crude will go to zero. It will go to zero because we will solve energy at the fision fusion.
